coredata.dc:title: Gram-scale synthesis of graphene quantum dots via energetic material deflagration for bioimaging application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Physics and Chemistry of Solids
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Jialin
Liu, Yousong
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots, which possesses unique photoluminescence property, have received enormous attention for bio-sensor, photovoltaic devices and bioimaging application. However, synthesizing high-quality of GQDs remains a formidable obstacle in terms of the two conventional methods since the “top-down” method usually requires expensive raw materials while the “bottom-up” method suffers from incompatibility between quality and production. Thus, developing new strategies to trade-off these issues is promising for GQDs production. Here, we reported an ultrafast deflagration strategy by igniting Al and pentafluoropyridine mixture to prepare high-quality of carbon nanomaterials (CNs) with high graphitization, reaching a high yield of ∼3 g. Combining with a subsequent Hummer's exfoliation and dialyze method, the CNs materials were successfully exfoliated into the uniform-dispersed graphene quantum dots (D-GQDs) with size of 1∼2 nm. The as-prepared D-GQDs derivatives shows remarkable optical properties and it is successfully applied in fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells. Our results in this study highlighted the high efficiency of deflagration technique to prepare functional carbon-based nanomaterials, and proposed a facile and fast strategy for gram-scale synthesis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Deflagration
Carbon nanomaterials
Bioimaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of CNs
Morphology and optical property of D-QGDs
Demonstration of Hela cells imaging with incubation of D-GQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province ( 2023NSFSC1075 ) and Postdoc Fellow Special Funding of Sichuan Province ( W03019023601004268 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) and (b) SEM images of the as-prepared CNs.

TEM images of the as-prepared CNs by deflagration reaction. (a)–(b) TEM images of CNs presents carbon nanospheres with the particles size varying from 100 to 200 nm; (c)–(f) HRTEM image of CNs presents clear graphite layers numbers.

(a) XRD pattern of the CNs; (b) Raman spectra of the CNs; (c) XPS full spectrum of the CNs; (d) High-resolution XPS C 1s spectra of the CNs; (e) High-resolution XPS N 1s spectra of the CNs; (f) Three types of N composition doped in graphite.

(a)–(c) HRTEM images of the D-GQDs with uniform distribution of graphene quantum dots; (d) AFM images of the D-GQDs (inset: the height profile of line scan in the image); (e) The absorption and fluorescence (400 nm excitation) spectra of the D-GQDs; (f) Photoluminescence (PL) emission at various excitation wavelengths of the D-GQDs (excitation wavelength from 500 to 620 nm).

Three random regions of demonstration of living Hela cells imaging of D-GQDs. (a) Bright field images of pristine Hela cells without incubation; (b) Fluorescence images of Hela cells with incubation in D-GQDs for 24 h, green laser excitation is showing localization of the D-GQDs into the cell nucleus; (c) Merged fluorescence image of the living Hela cells.
Cytotoxicity evaluation tests of Hela cells with different volume of D-GQDs from 1 to 20 μL for 24 h incubation.
Fabrication method for the synthesis of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Gram-scale synthesis of graphene quantum dots via energetic material deflagration for bioimaging application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, 611731, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials and Energy, University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, 611731, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemical Materials China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang, 621900, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Chemical Materials China Academy of Engineering Physics

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemical Materials China Academy of Engineering Physics, Mianyang 621900, P.R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots, which possesses unique photoluminescence property, have received enormous attention for bio-sensor, photovoltaic devices and bioimaging application. However, synthesizing high-quality of GQDs remains a formidable obstacle in terms of the two conventional methods since the “top-down” method usually requires expensive raw materials while the “bottom-up” method suffers from incompatibility between quality and production. Thus, developing new strategies to trade-off these issues is promising for GQDs production. Here, we reported an ultrafast deflagration strategy by igniting Al and pentafluoropyridine mixture to prepare high-quality of carbon nanomaterials (CNs) with high graphitization, reaching a high yield of ∼3 g. Combining with a subsequent Hummer's exfoliation and dialyze method, the CNs materials were successfully exfoliated into the uniform-dispersed graphene quantum dots (D-GQDs) with size of 1∼2 nm. The as-prepared D-GQDs derivatives shows remarkable optical properties and it is successfully applied in fluorescence imaging of HeLa cells. Our results in this study highlighted the high efficiency of deflagration technique to prepare functional carbon-based nanomaterials, and proposed a facile and fast strategy for gram-scale synthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon based luminescent nanomaterials (CLNMs), due to their photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity and high biocompatibility properties, have aroused wide attention in application of biomedical field [1–3]. Among the various CLNMs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) stand out because of their superior photostability properties, long-term tracking ability and adjustable long wavelength fluorescence [4,5]. To date, the synthetic approaches of GQDs are generally classified into two main categories: top-down and bottom-up [4,6–8]. Of which, the “top-down” method such as oxidative cleavage is used for high-quality and high-yield GQDs production [2,9,10]. However, the “top-down” methods generally involved in expensive raw materials such as graphene, fullerene, carbon nanotube, etc., making them unavailable for large-scale synthesis [3,6,7]. Alternative to “top-down” method, “bottom-up” method is more extensively used because of its simple operation and low-cost, such as chemical vapor deposition [11] and hydrothermal/solvothermal treatment, etc [12–14]. Whereas, these “bottom-up” strategies typically require long-time synthesis of several hours under a high temperature and pressure [15]. Moreover, substantial impurities and low production yield (milligrams) of “bottom-up” method further limits its extensive practical. In this regard, exploring a new way to trade-off the above weaknesses is of great interest for CLNMs synthesis.

Deflagration synthesis, as a type of “top-down” method, endows gram-scale synthesis of high-quality of carbon nanomaterials [16–20]. For example, Yang et al. reported a fast deflagration method by igniting NaN3 to prepare the defective g-C3N4, which exhibited pronounced performance in photocatalysis in visible-light range [21]. They also found the localized deflagration strategy of NaN3 combustion is highly efficient to prepare functional porous nanocarbon materials, which shows excellent electrocatalytic performance in oxygen reduction reaction [22]. Inspired by these findings, this unique deflagration method is believed to be a promising strategy to develop functional carbon materials for wide applications, and also making it possible to develop high-quality of raw carbon materials for preparing other low-dimension derivative materials. Herein, we present a novel deflagration technique by using Al powder as ignition agent for synthesizing low-cost, productive and high-quality of nanocarbon-based materials, which is potentially applicable as raw materials for CLNMs synthesis. During the deflagration process, pentafluoropyridine and Al powder, as the only two reactants, are mixing heated in a sealed stainless steel reactor to trigger combustion when the inner temperature reaches above 645 °C (the ignition point of Al). During seconds, gram-scale of carbon nanospheres with multi-layered graphite structure are formed (CNs), reaching a yield of ∼3 g. Followed by a subsequent Hummer's treatment and dialyze method, the as-prepared CNs products were successfully exfoliated into the final gram-level graphene quantum dots (D-GQDs). The obtained D-GQDs exhibits well-dispersed carbon dots with small sizes of 1∼2 nm. More importantly, the D-GQDs exhibits excellent optical emission properties in long wavelength region and it was successfully applied in Hela cell imaging. We propose this novel deflagration technique provides a fast and low-cost approach for gram-scale synthesis of carbon nanomaterials.

In a typical process, 20 g pentafluoropyridine (C5F5N) and 15 g Al powder were mixed together and stirred for 2 h, then dry the mixture in a vacuum oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Subsequently, the dried mixture was sealed in a stainless steel reactor for ignition. The stainless steel reactor is equipped with a temperature heater for heating and an manometer to monitor whether a reaction occurs. After heating up to 700 °C (heating rate of 10°/min) and maintained for nearly 2 min until the deflagration occurs. The moment of deflagration is observed from barometer as the pressure is shown to rise sharply, which is caused by the releasing of sublimated AlF3 (byproducts) and N2 gas. Then turn off the heater and cool down the reactor naturally, collect the black-color products and wash them by 200 mL NaOH solution (1 M) at 60 °C overnight to remove the excess Al. The product is labeled by CNs (carbon nanomaterials).

Hummer's method and dialyze method is applied to exfoliate the as-prepared CNs into the final GQDs products [23,24]. In a typical process, the as-prepared 1 g CNs products were added to the 34 mL of 1 M H2SO4 at 0° C, then 0.75 g of NaNO3 was added into the mixture. Subsequently, 5 g KMnO4 was added into the mixture solution under vigorous stirring in an ice bath. The solution was further stirred at 35 °C for 2 h, and then add 200 mL deionized H2O into the solution. After further stirring for 15 min, adding 4 mL H2O2 (30%) into the solution to suspend the reaction. Finally, centrifuge the above product to remove the insoluble impurities (AlF3) and dialyze it in a dialysis bag overnight. The product is labeled as D-GQDs that the letter D represents the word of deflagration.

The cells were cultured and maintained in DMEM (Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium) fixed with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin streptomycin in a 5% CO2 environment. 293A cells were plated at 4 × 103 cells per well on, and were left to adhere overnight. The cells were then incubated in the presence of D-GQDs with different volume of (0–20 μL:0/5/10/15/20 μL, 80 μg/mL) at 37 °C for 24 h in triplicate of 96 well plates. Next, wash the cells with PBS buffer (200 μl, 37 °C) for three times under sterile condition. Then 200 μL of [3-(4,5-dimethyl-2-thiazolyl)-2, 5-diphenytetrazolium bromide (4 mg/ml)] (MTT) was added into per well and incubated for additional 4h. Finally, the incubated cells with D-GQDs were treated with 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) per well for 5 min further incubation at RT. Optical absorbance of the suspension was measured at 570 nm in a Tecan Infinite M200 microplate reader.

For imaging, 293A cells were seeded at a density of 1x104 cells/cm2 in sterile chamber slides (Nunc, USA) with 500 μL culture medium. After culture for 12 h, 60 μg/mL D-GQDs was added into culture medium for incubation under sterile conditions. After 4 h of culture, the cells were washed 3-5 times with PBS to remove medium with D-GQDs. Cells were set with paraformaldehyde (1%) and Vectashield antifade mounting media with DAPI. The fluorescence images were acquired by Nikon Eclipse 90i microscope (Photometrics, AZ) at 460 nm excitation. Nikon Intensilight C-HGFI lamp were used as the fluorescence light source.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was applied to show the morphology and particle size by FEI Nova Nano450. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution TEM were conducted to demonstrate the graphite structure by FEI F30 with an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. Atomic Force Microscope (AFM) was performed on SPM-9600 to observe the size of carbon dots. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was used to detect the crystal structure by Bruker D8 ADVANCE. Raman spectra was performed by a Renishaw inVia 2000. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to analysis the composition and chemical bond by K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher, America) with the accelerated voltage of 5 kV. Ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) was conducted on Shimadzu UV-3600 to detect the optical property. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were applied by Varian Cary Eclipse spectrophotometer to monitor the fluorescence property.

In our strategy, the deflagration flame plays a critical role in the formation of the CNs. A custom-made stainless steel reactor, equipped with a manometer, is used for triggering deflagration, which can easily withstand high temperature (1200 °C) and high pressure (10 MPa) to assure security. Al powder, as one of the reactants, acts as the energetic source for ignition (combustion point: 645 °C). Another reactant of pentafluoropyridine (C5F5N), due to its abundant carbon and simple composition, is used as carbon source. Moreover, its basic structure of carbon ring has advantages in forming ordered-array graphite carbon. After continuous heating for minutes (above 645 °C), the deflagration reaction occurs with a rapid rise of pressure (>10 MPa) and temperature (>1000 °C). The whole deflagration reaction is completed within seconds, which offers a high temperature/pressure condition needed for growing highly graphitization of carbon nanomaterials. Here, we propose the deflagration reaction as below:
Al + C5F5N → C + N2 ↑ + AlF3 ↑ ΔfH0 = −1510 kJ/mol

The byproduct of AlF3 gas is formed due to sublimation during the deflagration process, and it will then cool down as a solid island to offer residual heat for carbon growth. The as-prepared product (CNs) from this deflagration process is fluffy and productive, reaching a high yield of ∼3 g. Finally, the obtained CNs were exfoliated into small-sized graphene quantum dost (D-GQDs) using simple Hummer’ method and dialyze method. We presented the synthesis illustration of D-GQDs in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and structure analysis of CNs
ce:section.ce:para: The preliminary CNs materials, formed from the momentary deflagration reaction, are composed by carbon nanosphere, as revealed by scanning electron microscope (SEM) in Fig. 2 a. The diameter sizes of these nanospheres are distributed from 150 to 200 nm and the surfaces show unique pipe-shaped channels, implying its porous features (Fig. 2b). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images shows these carbon nanospheres have clear shells with thickness varying from 15 to 20 nm (Fig. 3 a–b), which might be caused by the presence of AlF3 as an island for free carbon wrapping and growing on the outer space. Specifically, the high resolution TEM (HRTEM) images in Fig. 3c–f shows these nanospheres have diverse carbon layers ranging from 2 to 18 layers, while 8–10 layers of nanospheres are the most, suggesting its multi-layered structure. Notably, the lattice fringe space of these carbon layers is observed to be 0.3 nm, in accordance with the (002) interplanar of graphene, confirming the graphite structure [25,26]. Typically, traditional calcination requires several hours’ annealing to form multi-layered graphite (>10 layer), while the deflagration method is noticeable to make it during several minutes (including heating time). We speculate the ultrafast synthesis of CNs materials is benefit from the extreme condition of high temperature and pressure, while it still requires more in-depth understanding of their formation mechanism.

XRD analysis was carried out to detect the crystal structure of the CNs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the three diffraction peaks centered at 24.5°, 41° and 51° are assigned to the lattice plane of (002), (200) and (004) of graphite, confirming its highly crystalline structure [27–29]. Notably, some additional peaks with weak intensity are identified to be AlF3 byproducts, which is quite difficult to remove completely due to they are positioning at the core site of nanospheres. Raman spectra further verified its highly graphite structure. As shown in Fig. 4b, three peaks were observed at 1350 cm−1 and 1582 cm−1, which were belongs to D-band and G-band, respectively [30–32]. The D band generally attributed to the defects and disorders in the form from breathing mode of k-point phonons of sp3 carbon [32]. The G band is assigned to the in-plane vibration of sp2 bonds of carbon atoms, reflecting the degree of graphitization [32,33]. The integral intensity ratio of D and G bands (ID/IG) was calculated to be 0.42 (<1), which indicates high degree of graphitization of the CNs. Notably, the peak of 2695 cm−1 indicates the presence of 2D band [34,35]. Previous studies suggest that the integral intensity ratio of 2D and G bands (I2D/IG) reflects the thickness of graphite layers. For example, the I2D/IG of single-layer graphene is measured to be 2, while the smaller value means thicker graphite layers [36]. Here, the I2D/IG of the CNs was measured to be 0.95. These results again confirm its multi-layered feature of CNs.

The composition and chemical bond of the CNs was studied by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The full spectra (inset of Fig. 4c) shows three peaks at 285, 396 and 535 eV, belonging to C, N and O element, respectively [37]. This result indicates a pronounced C 1s peak, suggesting the CNs materials are mainly composed by carbon element. The minimal N element comes from the decomposed C5F5N precursor that the free N atoms participating in the carbon growth during deflagration, despite most of the N atoms are released by forming N2. Besides, the O element is regarded to be partially oxidized in air. The C 1s XPS spectra (Fig. 4d) can be fitted into three peaks at 284.6 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.5 eV, which are assigned to the sp2 carbon (C-C/C=C), the sp3 carbon (C-N) and the C=O in carboxyl group, respectively [31,38,39]. The dominant C-C/C=C species of XPS, along with the small ID/IG value of Raman, suggest its high purity of sp2 carbon of the CNs. It is noted that the CNs contains a small amount of nitrogen element, as shown in Fig. 4e–f, the composition of pyridinic N and pyrrolic N is significantly more than graphitic N [31], suggesting these N atoms were mostly distributed on the defects and edge sites in graphite plane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and optical property of D-QGDs
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain high-quality of CLNMs, the as-prepared CNs materials was exfoliated into GQDs (D-GQDs) using Hummer's method and dialyze method. As revealed by TEM in Fig. 5 a–b, D-GQDs sample shows well-dispersed and small carbon dots. The crystallinity of these carbon particles was verified by HRTEM (inset in Fig. 5c), the lattice spacing of 0.3 nm suggesting the typical (002) facet of graphite. Moreover, the atomic force microscope (AFM) in Fig. 5d reveals that the height sizes of these carbon particles are ranging from 1 to 2 nm, accorded with the typical size of graphene quantum dots. Table 1 shows that our strategy of deflagration and subsequent treatment, as a type of “top-down” method, is applicable to obtain smaller-sized GQDs compared with other reported common methods. The optical characteristic of D-GQDs are investigated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 5e, UV–Vis spectra of D-GQDs shows a wide absorption from 700 nm to 200 nm. This unique broad UV–vis absorption indicates that D-GQDs shows potential ability in optical application [4,40] Moreover, the PL excitation at 400 nm showed characteristic yellow fluorescence emission (centered around 590 nm, red line in Fig. 5e). PL spectra in Fig. 5f revealed when the excitation wavelength is changed, the intensity of PL emission decreases and shows a redshift from 550 to 650 nm, covering nearly visible light. It is reported that the PL excitation-dependent feature of GQDs is generally attributed to the quantum confinement, edge effect and surface emissive traps [7,41,42].These observations suggest the present D-GQDs prepared via deflagration reaction and exfoliation treatment have substantial traps or defect states, which might be caused by the introduced nitrogen element or oxygen-containing groups. Yet, the D-GQDs still exhibit multi-PL emission fluorescence, making them applicable in bioimaging field.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Demonstration of Hela cells imaging with incubation of D-GQDs

ce:section.ce:para: In summary, a facile and fast deflagration strategy was developed to synthesize graphite-based materials with gram-scale production. Combining with the subsequent Hummer's exfoliation and dialyze method, high-quality of graphene quantum dots (D-GQDs) was prepared. During the initial deflagration process, multiple-layered nanocarbon spheres (CNs) were produced with particle sizes from 150 to 200 nm and high graphitization, reaching a productive yield of ∼3 g. After Hummer's and dialyze treatment, the CNs was successfully exfoliated into well-dispersed graphene quantum dots with average size of 1∼2 nm (D-GQDs). Surprisingly, the prepared D-GQDs derivatives exhibit pronounced photoluminescent properties, it also shows excellent fluorescence bio-imaging ability of HeLa cells with good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity. Therefore, we propose that this ultrafast deflagration offers a promising way to achieve high-quality and gram-scale synthesis of functional carbon nanomaterials.
This research was funded by Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province (2023NSFSC1075) and Postdoc Fellow Special Funding of Sichuan Province (W03019023601004268).

J.T. and Y.L. conceptualized the project. Y.L. supervised the project. J.T. prepared the materials and performed the tests. J.T. drew the schematic diagram. Y.L. performed the cell-imaging experiment. J.T. wrote the paper. All authors discussed the results and commented on the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
